title: Simple Budgeting Basics
content: A simple way to manage money is to divide income into three broad parts: essential needs (food, rent, transport, school fees), savings (even a small fixed amount set aside regularly before spending), and other wants. Tracking spending for a few weeks, even on paper or in a notebook, helps reveal where money is actually going and makes it easier to spot areas to cut back.

title: Understanding Loan Interest Rates
content: Interest is the extra amount charged on top of a loan, usually shown as a percentage. A lower rate means less extra to repay. Loans described only by a naira amount added per week or month, without a clear annual percentage rate (APR), can hide extremely high effective interest -- always ask what the total repayment amount will be, and over what period, before agreeing to any loan.

title: National Health Insurance Authority (NHIA) for Self-Employed Nigerians
content: The National Health Insurance Authority (NHIA) runs the GIFSHIP programme (Group, Individual and Family Social Health Insurance Programme) for self-employed people, traders, artisans, and informal workers who are not covered through an employer. Enrolling requires a National Identification Number (NIN) and payment of an annual premium (individual and family rates apply, and rates can change -- confirm the current amount with an NHIA office). Once enrolled, members choose a Health Maintenance Organization (HMO) and a healthcare provider, and can access covered treatment without needing to pay the full cost out of pocket at the point of care.

title: Building a Savings Habit With Small Amounts
content: Saving does not require large amounts to be effective. Setting aside even a small, fixed amount regularly -- whether daily, weekly, or after every sale for a trader -- builds a habit and a cushion for emergencies over time. Mobile money savings wallets, cooperative societies (esusu/ajo), and registered microfinance banks all offer ways to save small amounts safely, generally safer than keeping large amounts of cash at home.

title: Using USSD Banking Codes Safely
content: Many Nigerian banks offer USSD codes (like dialing a short number starting with *) for balance checks, transfers, and airtime purchase without needing internet access. These are convenient for areas with poor network coverage, but the same safety rules apply as with any banking: never share the PIN used to complete a USSD transaction with anyone, and only use the official USSD code published by your own bank -- scammers sometimes trick people into dialing a lookalike code that redirects a transfer to the scammer's account.
